lt_name: Analyzing Models in U.S. Institutions and Behavior
coconstruction_stages: Show students a current news story about a U.S. institution and ask them to describe who has power and how decisions get made.; Introduce the three models by name and have students sort their observations into participatory, pluralist, or elite categories.; Guide students to articulate what each model looks like in practice by asking what evidence would prove each one is happening.; Co-create descriptors for each rubric level by asking students what they would need to do to show they understand all three models.
coconstruction_student_prompts: Who had a say in this decision, and how did their voices matter?; Which of these three models best explains what you just described—where everyone participates, where groups compete, or where a few decide?; What would you need to see or hear to prove that a participatory model is really at work here?; How would you explain to someone else the difference between these three models using examples from real institutions?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose anchor examples that show clear, contrasting instances of each model in recognizable U.S. institutions such as local government, Congress, or corporate boards; select examples where the model is evident enough that students can identify it without heavy scaffolding.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify or explain how participatory, pluralist, or elite models show up in U.S. institutions.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify one or two of the models with help, but my explanations are unclear or not quite accurate.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify all three models on my own and explain how one or two of them work in institutions I already know about.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify and explain how participatory, pluralist, and elite models show up in contemporary U.S. institutions and political behavior.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply model analysis to new institutional contexts or explain how multiple models operate at the same time.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name all three models and give a clear example of each one from a real U.S. institution?; Can I explain not just what each model is, but how it actually affects who gets power and how decisions are made?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to pick one current U.S. institution and describe who made a recent decision there.; Introduce one model at a time and ask the student to find one piece of evidence that matches it.
feedback_moves_emerging: Have the student practice identifying the third model by comparing it directly to the two they already know.; Ask the student to explain in their own words what one model means before moving to a new institution.
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge the student to explain the third model with the same detail they used for the first two.; Present an unfamiliar institution and ask which model best explains what is happening there.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to identify a situation where two models seem to operate at the same time and explain how.; Have the student analyze an institution they have never studied before using all three models.

coconstruction_stages: Show students primary source excerpts from ratification debates and ask them to identify what concerns delegates were raising.; Guide students to match each concern to a specific compromise or Bill of Rights promise that addressed it.; Have students explain in their own words why each compromise or promise was necessary to move ratification forward.; Introduce the distinction between describing what a compromise did and analyzing why it mattered for ratification.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What problem or disagreement do you see in this ratification debate excerpt?; Which compromise or Bill of Rights promise directly responds to that concern?; Why do you think delegates needed this compromise before they would vote to ratify?; How would ratification have gone differently without this compromise?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose one example showing a student simply naming a compromise without linking it to ratification (emerging level) and one showing a student explaining how a specific compromise resolved a ratification obstacle (competent level).
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to analyze how compromises or the Bill of Rights shaped ratification.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify one compromise or one ratification concern, but I do not yet connect them to each other.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify major compromises and explain what role they played in ratification, but I do not yet explain why they were necessary.
student_rubric_competent: I can analyze how specific compromises and the Bill of Rights shaped ratification debates and explain how they made amendment possible.
student_rubric_extending: I can analyze how compromises shaped ratification and evaluate what long-term effects they had on the Constitution.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name at least two compromises and explain how each one addressed a ratification concern?; Can I explain why delegates would not have ratified without these compromises or the Bill of Rights?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask: What was one thing delegates disagreed about during ratification?; Point to a primary source and ask: What compromise or Bill of Rights promise does this debate lead to?
feedback_moves_emerging: You identified a compromise; now find the ratification concern it solved.; Ask: Why would delegates have refused to ratify without this compromise?
feedback_moves_developing: You explained what the compromise did; now explain why it was necessary for ratification to succeed.; Ask: How would the ratification process have changed if this compromise had not been made?
feedback_moves_competent: You analyzed how compromises shaped ratification; now consider what effect they had on the Constitution long after ratification.; Ask: Did this compromise solve the problem permanently, or did it create new tensions later?

competency_cluster: cluster_10: Describing Political Principles and Institutions in Context
coconstruction_stages: Show students a scenario and ask them to spot anything related to government, rules, or how decisions are made.; Introduce the vocabulary: principles, institutions, processes, policies, and behaviors, and sort their observations into these categories.; Compare two scenarios of different complexity and discuss what makes identifying elements harder or easier.; Build the five levels together by asking what 'no attempt,' 'with help,' 'on your own in familiar cases,' 'on your own everywhere,' and 'seeing connections' would look like.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What do you see in this scenario that has to do with government, rules, or how people make decisions?; Which of these words fit what you found: principles, institutions, processes, policies, or behaviors?; Can you identify the same kinds of political elements in a completely different scenario?; What would it look like if someone could not identify anything at all versus someone who could spot connections between different political elements?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose one familiar scenario students know well and one unfamiliar or complex scenario so students see the difference between identifying elements in known versus new contexts. Ensure examples show at least two different categories from the five vocabulary terms.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify political principles or institutions in a scenario.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some political principles or institutions when someone helps me, but I might miss some or get some wrong.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify political principles and institutions on my own in scenarios I know well, but I am not consistent when the scenario is new or different.
student_rubric_competent: I can accurately identify political principles, institutions, processes, policies, and behaviors on my own in any scenario or context.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify political elements and explain how principles, institutions, and behaviors connect to each other even in complex or unfamiliar scenarios.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name at least two political principles, institutions, processes, policies, or behaviors in this scenario without help?; Do my identifications make sense in this context, or am I guessing?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point to one clear political element in the scenario and name it aloud, then ask the student to find one more.; Ask a yes-or-no question to guide attention to a principle or institution in the scenario.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to sort their identifications into the five categories and check if each one fits.; Provide a second familiar scenario and ask the student to find the same type of element without support.
feedback_moves_developing: Present a scenario that is slightly different from ones the student has seen and ask them to identify elements using the same vocabulary.; Ask the student to explain why an element they identified counts as a principle, institution, process, policy, or behavior.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to identify a relationship or connection between two political elements in the scenario.; Present a complex scenario with multiple overlapping political elements and ask the student to map how they influence each other.

competency_cluster: cluster_10: Describing Political Principles and Institutions in Context
coconstruction_stages: Show students a political scenario and ask them to identify what principle or institution is at work.; Guide students to distinguish between naming something and actually describing what it does in that specific situation.; Have students articulate why that principle or institution matters in the scenario—what difference does it make.; Introduce the idea of seeing the same principle play out across different contexts and connecting it to larger systems.; Co-author level descriptors together, using student language to capture what each level looks like in action.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What political principle or institution do you see operating in this scenario?; How would you explain what that principle or institution actually does here—not just its name, but how it works in this specific situation?; Why does this principle or institution matter in this context—what would be different without it?; Can you think of another scenario where this same principle shows up, and does it matter in the same way or differently?; What bigger pattern or system does this principle connect to—how does it fit into how government or politics works overall?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that are concrete and relatable to students, where the principle or institution is clearly visible but not trivial. Select one scenario that shows a principle in a familiar context and another that shows the same principle in a contrasting context, so students can see how context changes significance.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to describe a political principle or institution.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name a political principle or institution, but with help, and my description does not yet show how it works in the specific situation or is not quite accurate.
student_rubric_developing: I can describe a political principle or institution in a specific situation on my own, but I do not fully explain why it matters or how it connects to the bigger picture.
student_rubric_competent: I can describe political principles, institutions, processes, policies, or behaviors in a specific situation and explain why they matter in that context.
student_rubric_extending: I can describe principles and institutions across different situations and connect their significance to larger patterns in how government and politics work.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I name the principle or institution AND explain what it actually does in this situation, not just define it?; Did I explain why this principle or institution matters—what difference does it make in this context?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point to a specific moment in the scenario and ask: what rule, role, or idea is being used here?; Offer two possible principles or institutions and ask the student to choose which one fits and why.
feedback_moves_emerging: Acknowledge the principle or institution named, then ask: what does it actually do in this scenario—how does it change what happens?; Provide a sentence starter: 'In this situation, [principle] works by…' and have the student complete it with details from the scenario.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask: why would this scenario look different or feel different if this principle or institution were not there?; Prompt: can you find another scenario where this same principle shows up, and does it matter in the same way?
feedback_moves_competent: Ask: what larger system or pattern does this principle connect to—how does it fit into the way government is designed overall?; Invite: can you trace this principle across two or three different contexts and show how its significance shifts?

competency_cluster: cluster_11: Explaining Political Arguments and Perspectives
coconstruction_stages: Show students a short argument or perspective and ask them to underline or highlight words that sound political.; Guide students to sort their highlighted words into categories: principles, institutions, processes, policies, or behaviors.; Have students name the political concept each category represents and discuss why it matters to the argument.; Work together to describe what identifying political concepts means and what it looks like at different levels of skill.; Test the emerging rubric level by giving students a new argument and asking them to identify concepts with a partner.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What words or ideas in this argument sound like they belong to government, law, or how society is organized?; Sort the words you found into groups: Are they about rules and values, organizations, how things work, specific decisions, or actions people take?; What is the name of the political concept in each group, and why did the author mention it?; What does it mean to identify a political concept, and how would you know if you did it well or not so well?; Can you find and name the political concepts in this new argument without my help?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose arguments that contain one or two clear political concepts at the emerging level and three or more interconnected concepts at the competent level; ensure concepts span different categories so students see the range of what counts as political.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify political concepts in an argument.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name one political concept from an argument when someone helps me, but my explanation is unclear or not quite right.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently identify one or two political concepts in an argument but I miss some or do not fully understand how they work.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify the political principles, institutions, processes, policies, or behaviors that an argument addresses.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify multiple political concepts in an argument and explain how they connect to each other and to the author's main point.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I name a political concept from the argument, and can I explain what it means?; Did I find all the political concepts the author mentioned, or did I miss some?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point to a specific phrase in the argument and ask: Is this about government, rules, institutions, or how society works?; Model naming one political concept aloud, then ask the student to name a different one from the same argument with your support.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to explain why the concept they named matters to the argument's main idea.; Guide them to find a second political concept by asking: What else does the author talk about that relates to government or society?
feedback_moves_developing: Ask: Are there other political ideas or institutions the author mentions that you have not named yet?; Have the student explain how each concept they identified connects to the argument's central claim.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to show how two or more of the concepts they identified relate to or support each other.; Challenge them to explain what would change in the argument if one of the political concepts were removed.

competency_cluster: cluster_11: Explaining Political Arguments and Perspectives
coconstruction_stages: Show students a short argument or perspective from a political source and ask them to name any political concepts they notice.; Guide students to distinguish between simply naming a concept and actually explaining how the argument connects to it.; Have students practice writing 'how' and 'why' sentences that show the relationship between an argument and a concept.; Collect student-generated examples at different quality levels and sort them together into the five rubric levels.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What political concept, principle, or institution does this argument touch on?; How does the argument use or relate to that concept—what is the connection?; Why does the author make this argument—what about the concept makes it matter to their point?; Is your explanation clear enough that someone who doesn't know the concept could understand the link?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose arguments that clearly connect to recognizable concepts like separation of powers, representation, or due process, and select student work that shows the full range from naming only to explaining both how and why the relationship exists. Avoid examples that require specialized prior knowledge.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to connect an argument to a political concept.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name a political concept that an argument relates to, but I need help explaining how they connect.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain how an argument connects to a political concept on my own, but I may miss deeper reasons or details.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain both how and why an argument or perspective relates to a specific political principle, institution, process, policy, or behavior.
student_rubric_extending: I can explain argument-to-concept relationships across multiple sources and spot tensions when different principles or perspectives compete.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I explain not just what concept the argument touches, but how it connects and why that connection matters?; Could someone who hasn't read the source understand the relationship between the argument and the political concept from my explanation?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to point to one word or phrase in the argument that sounds political, then name what concept or institution it relates to.; Provide a concept and ask the student to find one sentence in the argument that shows that concept at work.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to add one sentence explaining how the argument uses or depends on the concept they named.; Prompt: 'Why would the author care about this concept—what does it help them prove or show?'
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to explain not just how but also why the author chose to appeal to that concept in their argument.; Encourage the student to add a detail about what the concept means or how it works to deepen the explanation.
feedback_moves_competent: Invite the student to find a second source with a different argument and explain how it relates to the same or a different concept.; Ask the student to identify what happens when two political principles or perspectives in different arguments pull in opposite directions.

competency_cluster: cluster_12: Applying Political Principles to Scenarios
coconstruction_stages: Show students a political principle and a real-world scenario, then ask them to identify what the principle means and where they see it in action.; Guide students to distinguish between incomplete attempts and clear, accurate explanations of how the principle works in that scenario.; Have students generate their own scenarios and test whether they can apply the principle independently without teacher prompts.; Introduce a second, contrasting scenario and ask students to explain how the same principle operates differently in each context.; Invite students to imagine a scenario where two principles might compete and discuss which one applies or how both matter.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does this political principle mean in your own words, and where do you see it happening in this scenario?; How would you explain to someone else exactly how this principle is working here—what evidence shows it?; Can you come up with your own example where this principle applies, and explain why it fits?; How does this principle work differently in this new scenario compared to the first one?; What would happen if two different principles pulled in opposite directions in the same situation?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that are concrete and relatable to students' lives or recent events, and select one that shows incomplete or surface-level reasoning alongside one that demonstrates clear, accurate application with depth. Avoid scenarios that are too abstract or require specialized knowledge beyond the course.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to explain how a political principle applies to a scenario.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify a political principle and name it, but my explanation of how it works in a scenario is incomplete or contains errors.
student_rubric_developing: I can explain how a political principle applies to a scenario on my own, but my reasoning is basic and may not work well in unfamiliar situations.
student_rubric_competent: I can clearly explain how political principles, institutions, processes, or policies apply to different scenarios with accurate and thoughtful reasoning.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply principles across multiple scenarios, connect them together, and evaluate which interpretation or principle matters most in complex situations.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain not just what the principle is, but exactly how it is working in this scenario with specific details?; Does my explanation make sense in more than one scenario, or does it only work in the one example I practiced?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to name the political principle first, then point to one place in the scenario where they think it appears.; Model one complete explanation of how a principle applies, then ask the student to try the same process with a different principle.
feedback_moves_emerging: Acknowledge the principle they identified, then ask them to add one more detail: what specifically is happening that shows this principle at work?; Provide a sentence starter: 'This principle matters here because…' and have them complete it with evidence from the scenario.
feedback_moves_developing: Present a second scenario and ask how the principle works differently there, pushing them to notice context and nuance.; Ask them to predict what would happen if one condition in the scenario changed, and explain how the principle would still apply.
feedback_moves_competent: Introduce a scenario where two principles might apply and ask them to explain which one is more important or how both shape the outcome.; Challenge them to find a real-world example where the principle operates in a way they did not expect, and explain why.

competency_cluster: cluster_12: Applying Political Principles to Scenarios
coconstruction_stages: Show students a short scenario or image involving government action and ask them to spot what political idea or institution is at work.; Guide students to name and define the principles and institutions they noticed, building a shared list on the board.; Have students sort their list into categories and discuss what makes an identification complete versus incomplete.; Work together to write what each level looks like, using student language and their own examples from the scenario.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What political principle or institution do you see happening in this scenario?; How do you know that is a principle or institution and not just something someone did?; What details would you need to include to make your identification complete and accurate?; When would it be hard to identify a principle or institution, and what would help you do it anyway?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that show one clear principle or institution at work and one that is muddled or partial so students can compare what a complete identification looks like versus an incomplete one. Include both familiar contexts and one slightly unfamiliar context to help students see the range of the rubric.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify any political principles or institutions.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some political principles or institutions when someone helps me, but my identification is missing details or is not quite right.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify political principles and institutions on my own in situations I know well, but I might miss some parts or need to ask for clarification.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify political principles, institutions, U.S. Constitution processes, and policies on my own in any scenario, and my identification is accurate and complete.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify political principles and institutions even in new or complicated scenarios and explain how they connect to each other.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I name the specific principle or institution, or did I just describe what happened?; Can I explain why what I identified is a principle or institution and not just a single action?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point to a clear example of a principle or institution in action and ask the student to name what they see.; Offer two choices of principles or institutions and ask which one matches the scenario.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to add one more detail to their identification to make it more complete.; Prompt them to check their answer against the definition of the principle or institution they named.
feedback_moves_developing: Present a scenario slightly different from the ones they have practiced and ask them to identify the principle or institution without support.; Ask them to explain the connection between the principle or institution and the U.S. Constitution or a specific policy.
feedback_moves_competent: Give them a complex scenario with multiple principles or institutions at work and ask them to identify all of them.; Ask them to explain how two different principles or institutions in the scenario relate to or support each other.

coconstruction_stages: Show students a list of 5–6 governmental terms and ask them to sort by how well they already know each one.; Have students work in pairs to write what they think each term means, then share out and discuss accuracy.; Introduce the five rubric levels and ask students to match sample student work to each level.; Guide students to refine level descriptors together so they reflect what 'good' looks like at each stage.; Have students identify which terms they can define confidently now and which ones need more practice.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does this governmental term mean in your own words, and how confident are you in that definition?; Which of these definitions is most complete and accurate, and what makes it better than the others?; If you were teaching a younger student this term, what would you say to help them understand it?; Can you think of a real example from U.S. government where this term is used?; What details or parts of the definition do you think are most important to remember?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose student work samples that show a clear progression from vague or partial definitions to precise, complete ones; include one example where a student correctly defines a term but struggles with a different one to show that competence is about the whole set of essential terms.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify or define governmental terms.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some governmental terms and attempt definitions, but they are incomplete or not quite right, and I need help.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify and define most of the essential governmental terms accurately on my own when the context is familiar.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify and state accurate definitions of all the essential governmental terms used in U.S. civics.
student_rubric_extending: I can use governmental term definitions to explain how different concepts connect or to clarify how institutions work together.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I define each essential governmental term accurately without looking at my notes?; Do my definitions use clear language that someone else could understand?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Start with one governmental term and ask the student to repeat it back and say one thing they notice about it.; Provide a simple, student-friendly definition and ask the student to use it in a sentence about U.S. government.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask the student to compare their definition with the accurate one and identify what part is missing or incorrect.; Have the student define the term again using a sentence frame or visual aid to make it more complete.
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge the student to define an unfamiliar governmental term using what they know about similar terms.; Ask the student to explain how two related terms are different from each other.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to show how two governmental terms relate to each other or work together in U.S. institutions.; Have the student use multiple governmental terms together to explain a real situation in government.
